results.possibleIdentity: Frank T. Turner (1911–?) was an American electronics engineer who spent most of his professional career at Western Union. He is best known in the art world for his collaboration with artist Wen-Ying Tsai on the "Cybernetic Sculpture" (1968). This work was awarded one of two second-place prizes in the international competition for engineers and artists organized by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). The sculpture, which consisted of stainless steel rods that vibrated in response to sound and were illuminated by stroboscopic lights, was featured in the landmark 1968–1969 Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) exhibition "The Machine as Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age." Turner's technical expertise in servos, digital electronics, and low-level electronics allowed him to help Tsai perfect a system where the sculpture could sense and respond to environmental sounds in real-time. Professionally, at Western Union, Turner contributed to the development of facsimile telegraph systems, holding patents such as US Patent 2,607,844 for bridge circuits used in facsimile transmission. In his later years, he reflected on the intersection of science and technology in publications like "Technology in Society."
